title: Revelation
author: John the apostle, exiled on the island of Patmos
setting: Written to seven churches in the Roman province of Asia Minor (modern Turkey) who were experiencing or facing persecution, economic pressure, and pressure to compromise with Roman imperial cult. John writes in the genre of apocalyptic prophecy.
purpose: To encourage persecuted churches that God is sovereign over history, that Christ has conquered through his death and resurrection, and that the enemies of God's people — Rome, the beast, the false prophet — will be defeated. The book calls the church to faithful endurance, worship, and witness.
themes: The sovereignty of God and the Lamb
The new creation: new heaven and new earth
Worship in heaven and on earth

outline.label: Prologue and Vision of the Risen Christ
The Heavenly Throne Room and the Sealed Scroll
The Dragon, the Beasts, and Babylon
The Return of Christ and Final Judgment
The New Creation: New Heaven and New Earth

before.label: Nero's persecution and the destruction of Jerusalem

after.label: End of the canon; the promise awaits fulfillment

key_people.name: Jesus Christ, the Lamb
key_people.role: The central figure of the entire vision — simultaneously the Lion of Judah (5:5), the slain Lamb (5:6), the rider on the white horse (19:11), and the bridegroom of the new Jerusalem (21:2, 9-10)

key_people.name: John the Seer
key_people.role: The prophet in exile on Patmos who receives and records the vision; his pastoral purpose is explicit: to comfort and embolden persecuted churches by showing them the ultimate outcome of history

key_people.name: The Seven Churches
key_people.role: Real congregations in Asia Minor (2:1-3:22) who are the letter's primary addressees; their specific situations (prosperity at Laodicea, poverty at Smyrna, compromise at Pergamum) establish that the book is pastoral as well as prophetic

key_people.name: The Beast and the False Prophet
key_people.role: Symbolic figures representing Roman imperial power and the imperial cult priesthood; their parallel with the Lamb (the beast has seven heads; the false prophet performs signs) is deliberately ironic

key_people.name: The Woman and the Whore
key_people.role: Two contrasting female figures: the woman clothed with the sun (the covenant community, ch. 12) and Babylon the Great (ch. 17-18), the city that seduces the nations — representing the church and the anti-church respectively

context: Revelation was written c. AD 95-96 by John of Patmos during the reign of Domitian, when emperor worship was being enforced in Asia Minor and Christians who refused to participate faced economic and social sanctions, and in some cases death. The book belongs to the genre of Jewish apocalyptic literature — using symbolic imagery, cosmic visions, and heavenly perspectives to interpret earthly reality. Its primary purpose is pastoral and political: to encourage Christians facing imperial pressure by showing them the true character of Rome (Babylon, the Beast), the cosmic status of Christ (Lord of lords, King of kings), and the certain outcome of history (the new Jerusalem).
christ_connection: Revelation is the NT's most comprehensive vision of the exalted, reigning, and returning Christ. He is Alpha and Omega (1:8; 22:13), the first and the last, the living one who died and is alive forevermore, holding the keys of death and Hades (1:18). The Lamb who was slain (5:6) is the book's central christological image: the one who conquered through weakness (crucifixion) now holds the seven seals of history and is worthy to open them. His seven-fold worthiness (5:12) is answered by creation's universal acclamation (5:13). The entire book moves from the Lamb's enthronement to his final coming, with every seal, trumpet, and bowl serving the single purpose of bringing history to its appointed end: the marriage supper of the Lamb and the New Jerusalem.

key_verses.note: 'I am the Alpha and the Omega... who is and who was and who is to come, the Almighty' — Christ's self-identification that frames the entire book: he governs all of time and history
The new song: 'You were slain, and by your blood you ransomed people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation' — the Lamb's worthiness grounded in his atoning death
'Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man... He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more' — the final consummation; God's covenant promise perfectly fulfilled
'Surely I am coming soon. Amen. Come, Lord Jesus!' — the book's final prayer, the church's response to the vision: not escape from history but the return of the one who fills it

themes_detail.title: The Lamb Who Was Slain as Lord of History
themes_detail.text: The throne-room vision of Revelation 4-5 is the book's theological centre: God on the throne, surrounded by four living creatures and twenty-four elders, holds a sealed scroll that no one can open — until the Lamb who was slain takes it. His worthiness to open the scroll (to govern history) is grounded entirely in his crucifixion and resurrection: 'You were slain, and by your blood you ransomed people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation' (5:9). The entire subsequent vision (seals, trumpets, bowls) unfolds the Lamb's governance of history toward its consummation. Crucifixion is not the prelude to power; it is its exercise.

themes_detail.title: The Beast, Babylon, and the Critique of Empire
themes_detail.text: Revelation provides the NT's most sustained theological critique of political power that claims ultimate allegiance. Rome is Babylon — the great whore drunk with the blood of the saints (17:6), the city whose merchants and rulers mourn her fall (ch. 18), the empire that demands worship and kills those who refuse. The beast parodies the Lamb — it too has been wounded and lives (13:3), it too has a mark for those who belong to it, it too exercises authority over all nations. The polemic is not anti-government per se (Rom 13 acknowledges legitimate governing authority) but against the idolatrous claim that any empire can demand the absolute loyalty that belongs only to God and the Lamb.

themes_detail.title: The Suffering Church and Its Vindication
themes_detail.text: Revelation was written to comfort churches under pressure, and its vision of heavenly reality is designed to reframe the experience of earthly suffering. The martyrs under the altar cry out for justice (6:9-11) and are told to wait a little longer. The souls in white robes (7:13-17) have come out of the great tribulation; they are before God's throne because the Lamb is their shepherd. The beast makes war on the saints and overcomes them (13:7) — but the Lamb wins in the end. The pastoral argument is that appearances deceive: what looks like Rome winning is Rome condemned; what looks like the church losing is the church following the Lamb on the path to glory.

themes_detail.title: The New Jerusalem as the Consummation of All Things
themes_detail.text: Revelation 21-22 describes not heaven as an escape from earth but the new creation as the transformation of heaven and earth together: 'Behold, I am making all things new' (21:5). The new Jerusalem comes down from heaven to earth; the throne of God is on the earth; the nations bring their glory into the city. The final vision is not ethereal but earthy — a city with dimensions and walls and gates, watered by the river of life and shaded by the tree of life (22:1-2), whose leaves are for the healing of the nations. Every OT image of blessing — garden, city, temple, bride, marriage feast — converges in this final scene. History ends not in dissolution but in consummation.
